title: Expect 100-continue, Early Responses, Body Rejection, and Draining · Execution Trace: Continue Timing
summary: A server reads a huge body before authentication, sends 100 after a final response, drains attacker bytes without bounds, and lets client and server wait on each other forever. Planned artifact: Add and verify the continue timing increment in expectation and early-response coordination state machine. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Send 100 Continue only after header-level policy accepts receiving content and before a final response commits.
Parse Expect, support 100-continue deliberately, and return appropriate final failure for unsupported expectations.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Predict Continue Timing · Theory Continue Timing
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory protocol team handles case 8372 in a incident replay lab retaining minimized request-smuggling corpora. Build deterministic Go evidence to coordinate 100-continue and bounded early rejection without deadlock or unsafe reuse; preserve this operating constraint: a timeout must reserve cleanup time and retain the terminal phase; then append a second pipelined message and prove the first parser leaves the exact residual bytes. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Send 100 Continue only after header-level policy accepts receiving content and before a final response commits.
